does: This paper proposes Low-rank Knowledge Unlearning (LoKU), a parameter-efficient framework for removing sensitive data from large language models without full retraining. The method combines gradient ascent-based unlearning with low-rank adaptation, introducing two key innovations: an Inverted Hinge Loss that suppresses unwanted token generation while maintaining model fluency by boosting the next most likely token, and a data-adaptive LoRA initialization using low-rank approximation weighted by relative Fisher information. The approach aims to achieve better trade-offs between computational efficiency and generative performance compared to existing gradient ascent methods that suffer from unstable optimization and catastrophic forgetting.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in machine unlearning for neural networks, extending methods originally developed for smaller models to the challenging domain of large language models. It directly engages with limitations of Gradient Ascent unlearning approaches, which have become standard for removing content from LLMs but create instability issues. The paper also combines insights from low-rank adaptation techniques like LoRA, which have become popular for parameter-efficient fine-tuning, with unlearning objectives. This bridges two active areas: efficient adaptation methods and privacy-preserving machine learning, responding to growing concerns about copyright and privacy violations from LLM pretraining on web-scale data.
enables: This work enables more practical deployment of unlearning methods in production LLMs by reducing the computational burden and improving stability compared to full-model retraining or unstable gradient ascent approaches. The parameter-efficient framework could facilitate regular, iterative removal of copyrighted or private content without expensive recomputation. Future work could extend these techniques to handle more complex unlearning scenarios, develop better metrics for measuring successful unlearning without performance degradation, and explore whether data-adaptive initialization approaches could improve other parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods. The framework also opens directions for combining unlearning with other desiderata like alignment and robustness.
